title: Oval willow shopping basket
subtitle: A traditional oval base shopping basket woven in buff willow
excerpt: The oval shopping basket is the most practical form in the willow basketry tradition: a flat oval base, staked up with uprights, waled at the border, and fitted with a twisted handle. Buff willow (stripped, boiled, and dried) is the standard material; it bends without cracking after an overnight soak and holds its shape once dry. This intermediate project covers the oval slath base, staking up, randing the sides, and fitting a twisted rod handle.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for the willow basketry tradition and buff willow preparation. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for basket construction methods.

content.text: The oval shopping basket has been the standard market and garden basket in Britain since the mid-nineteenth century. It is made from
rods soaked overnight until they are supple enough to bend without cracking. The base is an oval
of crossed sticks woven round with short rods to build the oval floor; the uprights are staked up from the base border and the sides are built with
. The rim is tied off with a four-rod border. A twisted-rod or carved handle is fitted last.

For a 40 cm basket: 5 base sticks (30 cm long, 5 to 6 mm diameter, brown or white willow for stiffness), 60 to 80 weaving rods (60 to 90 cm long, 3 to 4 mm diameter, buff willow), 16 to 18 stakes (90 cm long, 4 to 5 mm diameter), and 2 handle bows (100 cm long, 8 mm diameter). Soak buff willow rods overnight in water, drain, and wrap in a damp cloth for 30 minutes before use. Soak thick sticks and handle bows for 4 to 6 hours longer.

A rapping iron to pack weavers down tight after each row. A sloyd knife to point stakes before inserting and to trim rod ends. Side cutters or heavy scissors for cutting rods to length (the sloyd knife will serve if these are not available). A damp cloth to keep worked rods supple.
Cut rod ends point away from the body. The rapping iron is struck downward; keep the free hand above the strike zone.

Take five base sticks. Pierce the three central ones through their midpoints with the knife point and thread the two side sticks through the slits to form a cross-shaped slath. Thread two weaving rods through the slath on either side and pair-weave (two rods chasing each other) around the slath arms for two rounds to lock the cross. Then open the arms out to the spacing needed for the oval: the longer axis should have three sticks, the shorter two, spaced to produce the target floor dimensions when woven out. Continue pair-weaving around the opened stakes until the base is 30 cm by 20 cm. Pack each row down with the rapping iron.

Point each side stake with the sloyd knife. Push one stake into the weave on either side of every base stick, angled downward into the base. Fold all stakes upright by bending each one over a thumb at the base-to-side join, then weave a four-rod wale around the full circumference to lock the uprights at the correct angle. This first wale defines the base border.

Insert a weaving rod behind any stake. Rand: over the next stake, behind the following one, over the next, continuing round the full circumference until the rod runs out. Insert the next rod behind the stake where the last ended and continue. Pack each round down with the rapping iron. Add a four-rod wale every 5 cm of height to reinforce the wall. Build the sides to 20 cm height.

The four-rod border finishes the rim. Take each upright stake in sequence: bend it down behind the stake to its right, then forward in front of the next two stakes, and tuck it down through the weave on the inside. The resulting plaited border locks every stake permanently and finishes the rim cleanly.

Push a handle bow through the weave at the centre point of each short side of the basket, driving it in 6 to 8 cm on each side. Wrap the bow with a thinner rod from one side to the other, overlapping the wraps tightly. Tuck the wrapping rod end into the weave on the far side. A second wrapping rod in the opposite direction fills any gaps between the first wrapping's turns.
A round basket uses the same techniques with a circular slath; the base sticks are crossed at the centre and opened out evenly in all directions.
White willow (stripped but not boiled) gives a pale natural surface. Brown willow (dried unstripped) gives a rustic tan-brown surface.
Spray the finished basket with water occasionally to keep the willow supple and prevent brittleness. Store out of direct sunlight; UV bleaches the buff colour.

items.fix: Pack the weave tighter on the convex side with the rapping iron. For future baskets, use stiffer sticks (6 mm diameter minimum) for bases over 25 cm.
items.cause: The base sticks are too thin for the base diameter, or the weaving tension was uneven.
items.symptom: The base buckles rather than lying flat

items.fix: Re-soak cracked rods in warm water for 30 minutes and rewrap in a damp cloth. Plan 12 to 16 hours of soaking for thick rods; shorter soaking works only for very thin material.
items.symptom: Rods crack as they are bent around the stakes
